description: Consistency-model-sensitivity detector table (port-loop-plan.md Phase 3; decomposition-schema.md §6.2). Curated, versioned, per-language/framework DATA — the sibling of conflict_detectors.json for the intent-CM tag. Each entry is a cheap, deterministic regex over source that FLAGS a place where the source silently assumes a central authority: ACID transactions, unique constraints, autoincrement/sequential ids, server-side access checks, or revision/locking. Every hit is a candidate for the LM adjudicator (intent_cm.py) to classify {hard | soft | none} — the table proposes a `cm_seed` prior, the strong model disposes. `cm_seed` is the mechanical prior severity; adjudication may downgrade a false positive to `none`. Adding a language/framework pattern is a table edit; adding a new detection engine is code (there is one engine: `regex`).
seed_note: CM-hard = cannot hold under eventual consistency without a chosen resolution strategy (unique/monotonic ids, single-writer locks). CM-soft = holds eventually, transient violation tolerable (access snapshots, revision history, conservation sums). CM-none = independent of consistency model. Seeds are priors: the mechanical layer over-flags (high recall), adjudication is the precision gate.

categories.transaction: ACID transaction boundary — a set of writes assumed atomic + isolated by a central store.
categories.unique-constraint: a uniqueness invariant enforced by the store at write time.
categories.autoincrement-id: reliance on a server-assigned sequential / monotonic id.
categories.access-check: a server-side access/permission check evaluated against authoritative central state.
categories.revision-lock: revisioning or explicit locking assuming a single serialized write history.

detectors.message: explicit ACID transaction boundary: a group of writes assumed atomic + isolated. Under eventual consistency there is no such boundary — the port must either move this to a coordination layer or make each write independently convergent.
transaction boundary — atomicity + isolation assumed. No central authority in the target: re-answer as a convergence rule or a coordination step.

field/entity uniqueness enforced by the store at write time. Two disconnected replicas can each satisfy it locally yet collide on merge — the port must choose a convergence rule (namespaced/UUID keys, or a coordination check).
declarative uniqueness key in config schema. A write-time invariant with no distributed analogue — re-answer on merge.

uniqueness constraint (ORM column / DDL). Central store enforces at write; a distributed target must converge or coordinate. NB: a dataframe `.unique()` dedup is NOT this — the pattern targets store-enforced uniqueness only.

reliance on a server-assigned sequential integer id (the entity's `id` key). Monotonic autoincrement is unavailable offline-first — the port must key on UUIDs (farmOS already carries a uuid entity key) and treat the serial id as a local, non-portable ordinal.
server-assigned sequential id. No monotonic authority in the target — key on a client-generated UUID.

server-side access check against authoritative central state (roles/permissions/grants). Offline-first, the deciding state may be stale — the port re-answers 'who may see what' in the selective-disclosure / sync layer, not as a synchronous central gate.
access hook — server-evaluated authorization. Re-express in the disclosure layer; a replica cannot assume the central grant table is current.
declarative access requirement on a route/permission. The source's answer to selective disclosure; the port re-answers it in the sync/disclosure layer rather than as a central request-time gate.
server-side authorization guard. In a local-first target, authorization state may be stale — move the decision into the disclosure/sync layer.

revisionable entity — a single serialized revision history with a server-assigned revision id. A distributed target has concurrent histories; preserve revisions as an eventual, merge-aware event log rather than a linear revision_id sequence.

explicit lock acquisition — assumes a single central lock manager serializing writers. No such manager exists across replicas; the port must make the guarded operation convergent or route it through a coordination layer.

changed-timestamp / optimistic-concurrency signal — the store rejects a write against a stale `changed` value. Under eventual consistency this becomes a merge decision (last-writer-wins or field-level CRDT), not a rejected write.
pessimistic row lock — a single-writer assumption. Re-answer as a convergence rule or coordination step.

framework_implied.description: Framework-level CM implications detected by filesystem presence (file globs, content patterns), not line-by-line regex. A detected framework implies the listed CM facts for the ENTIRE source tree — the implication IS the classification; no LM adjudication is performed. One row per (framework, category) is emitted with adjudication_source='framework-implied'. element_id pattern: 'framework-implied:{framework_id}:{category}'.
framework_implied.detection_note: Predicates are evaluated filesystem-wide. A 'file_glob' predicate checks for any matching file via rglob. A 'file_exists' predicate checks a path relative to source root. A 'file_content' predicate reads a matching file and checks for a regex pattern. Logic 'any' = OR over predicates.

predicates.description: Drupal module descriptor (.info.yml present means at least one Drupal module)

implied_facts.rationale: Drupal's database API (Database::getConnection()->startTransaction()) wraps all entity saves in PDO transactions; every hook_entity_presave/postsave pair runs inside an implicit transaction boundary — the framework assumes ACID atomicity from the central database.

Drupal entity types default to a SERIAL/autoincrement integer 'id' key assigned by the central database; any module that does not explicitly declare a UUID-only key inherits this server-assigned sequential id assumption.

Drupal's permission/role/grant system (node_access, hook_entity_access, route _permission) is evaluated synchronously at request time against a central grants table; all module access logic inherits this server-side authority assumption.

Drupal's revisionable content entity storage assigns monotonic revision_id values centrally via the database SERIAL; all revisionable entities assume a single serialized write history managed by the authoritative store.

predicates.description: Django management script at project root

implied_facts.rationale: Django's ORM wraps Model.save() and QuerySet bulk operations in database transactions by default; atomic() decorators and ATOMIC_REQUESTS=True reinforce that the framework assumes ACID atomicity from the central relational database.
Django models use AutoField (autoincrement integer) as the default primary key; any model without an explicit UUIDField primary_key=True inherits the serial-id assumption from the framework default.

Django's permission framework (has_perm, permission_required, DRF permission_classes) evaluates access synchronously against the central auth database; all view-level guards inherit the server-side authority assumption.

predicates.description: Rails or ActiveRecord listed in Gemfile

implied_facts.rationale: ActiveRecord wraps all Model.save / before_save / after_save callbacks in database transactions automatically; ActiveRecord::Base.transaction blocks make the assumption explicit — the framework requires ACID atomicity from the central relational database.

ActiveRecord generates autoincrement integer primary keys by default via t.primary_key in migrations; any model that does not override the primary key type inherits the server-assigned sequential id assumption.

Rails authorization gems (Pundit, CanCanCan, Devise) evaluate permissions synchronously against central state at request time; the framework ecosystem assumes server-side authority for all access decisions.
